title: Reviewing and Improving Our Narrative Writing
explanation_chunks: This page is all about looking back at the stories you've written, especially those that use flashbacks. You'll get to share your work with a partner and see what they think.

When you evaluate each other's stories, you'll think about if the story makes sense, if it's well-organized, and if the flashback moments are clear and helpful. You'll also check if interesting language tools, called figurative devices, were used.
You'll practice writing a review of a story, just like a professional book critic! This means giving your opinion on different parts of the story, like the characters and what happens.
Finally, you'll also get creative by designing a cover for your story, using art and design skills to show key events. This helps you think about how images can tell a story too.
examples: When evaluating a story, you might ask: 'Does the story move smoothly, or does it jump around too much?' or 'Is the flashback in the middle of the story easy to understand, or is it confusing?'
If you're writing a review of 'The Eye of the Wolf', you might say, 'The plot was exciting, but I felt the main character could have shown more bravery earlier on.'
key_points: Learn to give and receive constructive feedback on stories.
Understand how to evaluate story structure, flashbacks, and figurative language.
Practice writing critical reviews of stories, focusing on plot, characters, language, and themes.
Develop skills in visual storytelling through cover design and collage.
Reflect on your ability to use varied sentence structures, correct grammar, and effective punctuation.

vocabulary.definition: To judge or assess something carefully.
Organized or arranged in a particular way.
A scene in a story that takes the reader back to an earlier time.
Tools writers use to make their language more creative and impactful, like similes or metaphors.
The main events of a story, usually presented as a sequence.
The people, animals, or creatures in a story.
The main ideas or messages explored in a story.
A picture or design made by sticking various materials, like paper or photographs, onto a surface.
Conclusions reached on the basis of evidence and reasoning.
To read text and mark any errors in spelling, grammar, or punctuation.

explain_again: This page is like a big 'check-up' for your stories! You'll read your story aloud and get tips from a friend. You'll also learn how to write a review about a book and make a cool cover for your story. It's all about making your writing even better!
fun_analogy: This page is like being a chef who tastes their own cooking and gets friends to taste it too, to make sure it's super delicious. You're trying to make your story the tastiest dish ever!
